title: Dijon Pan-Sauce Pork Chops + Mashed Potatoes + Green Beans
ingredients: 1.5 lb Yukon gold or baby potatoes
2 tbsp sour cream or plain Greek yogurt (optional, for creamier mash)
Optional: lemon (a squeeze brightens the sauce)
instructions: Prep + boil potatoes: Wash potatoes. Cut into 1-inch chunks (leave skin on if you like). Put in a pot and cover with cold water by 1 inch. Add 1–2 tsp salt. Bring to a boil, then reduce to a steady simmer. Cook 12–15 minutes until a fork slides in easily.
Prep green beans: Rinse. Trim the stem ends (line up a handful and cut the tips, or snap off). Set aside.

Cook pork chops: Pat pork dry with paper towels (helps browning). Season both sides with salt and pepper. Heat a large skillet over medium-high heat with 1 tbsp oil. When the oil shimmers, add pork. Cook 3–5 min per side (boneless) until browned and cooked through. Doneness cue: juices run clear and the center is no longer pink; thermometer: 145°F, then rest. Transfer to a plate and tent loosely with foil.

Cook green beans (quick): In the same skillet (or a second pan), add green beans with a splash of water and a pinch of salt. Cover 3–4 minutes to steam, then uncover and cook 2–3 minutes until bright green and tender-crisp. (Alternative: microwave with a splash of water 3–4 minutes.)

Make Dijon pan sauce: Reduce heat to medium. Add 2 tbsp butter. Add 2 minced garlic cloves and cook 30 seconds (don’t let it brown). Sprinkle 1 tsp flour (optional) and stir 15 seconds. Pour in 1/2 cup broth while scraping up browned bits. Stir in 1 tbsp Dijon. Simmer 2–3 minutes until slightly thickened. Taste and add salt/pepper; a squeeze of lemon helps if it tastes heavy.

Mash potatoes: Drain potatoes well. Return to the hot pot (off heat) to evaporate steam 30 seconds. Mash with 1 tbsp butter and a splash of milk/water. Add sour cream/Greek yogurt if using. Season generously with salt and pepper.
Serve: Slice pork. Spoon Dijon sauce over pork. Plate with mashed potatoes and green beans.
Common mistakes to avoid: don’t skip drying pork (it won’t brown); don’t overcook pork—start checking early; don’t forget to taste the sauce and adjust salt/acid.
notes: Kid-friendly: serve sauce on the side. This reuses Dijon you already have.
